senses.definition: Having a seam or seams; stitched together.
examples: A seamed garment was neatly finished.
The seamed fabric held together well during washing.

senses.definition: Sailors; people who work on ships.
examples: The ship's crew included several seasoned seamen.
Seamen faced rough seas during the voyage.

senses.definition: A person or tool that creates seams; in manufacturing, a device that sews fabric along edges.
examples: The seamer stitched the fabric along the edge.
Industrial seamer machines run continuously.

senses.definition: Plural of seamer; devices or people who create seams.
examples: The factory has several seamers on the line.
Different seamers can produce different seam types.

senses.definition: Uncommon plural form; not widely used in modern English. Possibly a misspelling or archaic form related to seam.
examples: Historical texts mention seames in old tailor guild records.

senses.definition: A seabird also known as the lesser black-backed gull; genus Larus.
examples: A seamew wheeled above the harbor.
Seamews are common along rocky coasts.

senses.definition: Free of seams; sewn or manufactured without visible stitching.
examples: The seamfree fabric looks smooth under the light.
Seamfree designs reduce irritation for sensitive skin.

senses.definition: Morally low, disreputable, or sordid; often used for people or activities.
examples: That seamier part of town is best avoided after dark.
He told a seamier version of the story to intrigue listeners.

senses.definition: Most seamier; the most disreputable or dirty.
examples: The seamiest parts of the city pule with nightlife.
That was the seamiest plot twist in the book.

senses.definition: The quality of being seamier or having seams; can refer to moral dubiousness or physical seam quality.
examples: The seaminess of the report raised concerns.
The seaminess of the fabric affected its durability.

senses.definition: Plural of seaminess; rarely used in modern English.
examples: Scholarly works rarely discuss seaminesses in fabrics.

senses.definition: The act of making or joining a seam.
examples: Seaming is essential in garment construction.
The seam of the jacket shows even seaming.

senses.definition: Present participle of seam; to join with a seam.
examples: They are seam­ing two pieces of fabric together.
The tailor is seaming the edges.

senses.definition: Smooth and without visible seams; not separated or interrupted.
examples: Seamless skin is not common but there are ways to reduce seams.
The transition between scenes was seamless.

senses.definition: In a way that is smooth and without noticeable transitions or interruptions.
examples: The app integrates seamlessly with your calendar.
Operations run seamlessly despite the changes.

senses.definition: The quality of being seamless; smoothness without interruptions.
examples: The seamlessness of the user experience impressed users.
Design aims for seamlessness across devices.

senses.definition: Plural of seamlessness; rarely used in modern English.
examples: Some theoretical texts mention seamlessnesses.
In practice, seamlessnesses are described as design goals.

senses.definition: A small seam or a fringe; a tiny decorative seam.
examples: A delicate seamlet bordered the edge of the pocket.

senses.definition: Resembling a seam; having a line like a seam.
examples: The seamlike pattern ran across the fabric.
A seamlike crease appeared after washing.

senses.definition: Very close to being a seam; almost seamless or nearly at a seam.
examples: The fabric was seamost finished around the edges.

senses.definition: An underwater mountain rising from the sea floor.
examples: A seamount rises from the ocean floor, hosting diverse life.
Biologists study organisms around seamounts.

senses.definition: Plural of seamount; underwater mountains.
examples: Sea explorers mapped several seamounts.
Ecologists study ecosystems on seamounts.

senses.definition: An obscure or rare term; not widely attested in common English.
examples: References to seamrend are scarce.

senses.definition: An obscure or rare term; not widely attested in common English.
examples: The word seamrog appears in scarce dictionaries.

senses.definition: Lines where two pieces of fabric are sewn together; a stitched line.
examples: The seams on the shirt were zigzagged.
He repaired the seams with strong thread.

senses.definition: Third-person singular of seam; to join with a seam.
examples: She seams the two pieces of cloth.
The tailor seams the edges carefully.
